item.title: Louis Dauth Bakery and Confectionary
item.dc:creator: Nathaniel Dauth
item.description: The post Louis Dauth Bakery and Confectionary appeared first on Dauth Family Archive.
item.content:encoded:
Louis Dauth’s Bakery and Confectionary

233 Linden Street, Fort Collins

Louis Dauth was the eldest son of Andreas Dauth and the first of the Dauth siblings to immigrate to America in 1873. Here he located himself in Denver, where he undertook a variety of jobs including bar keeping, waiting, clerking and even starting a furniture business.1234 None of these endeavors appealed to Louis, and so he took a chance and set off for the newly founded town of Fort Collins in 1877.5 With just $250 to his name he opened a bakery “in a small one-story frame building” on the Mosman Block. Soon after he entered a business partnership with M. M. Zimmerman to create the Dauth & Zimmerman Bakery, located one door down from of the southwest corner of Linden and Jefferson.6 Despite this partnership only lasting a few months, Louis had found his calling. 

In August 1878 the first ever article about Louis’s bakery was published:

Louis Dauth, baker and confectioner, has purchased of Mrs. Harris, the building known as the “new store,” on Linden street, which he proposes to fit expressly for his business. He will erect an oven in the rear soon, and make some changes in the sales room to suit his rapidly increasing trade.7

Fort Collins Courier – August 10, 1878

Louis had just purchased the “New Store” a newly built property three doors down from of the southwest corner of Linden and Jefferson. He repurposed the establishment with an oven and sales room, and turned it into a proper bakery and grocery. Sales were good for Louis and so he started making improvements to his store. He put in a new flagstone sidewalk, added awnings above the shop windows, purchased a bakery wagon and hired a driver that would deliver “fresh bread, cakes, and pies” to whomever ordered.8910 By May 1880 Louis was confident with his business and announced his intentions to form a partnership with local jeweler E. W. Reed to build a whole business block across the street.11 However there was still much work ahead and construction was not expected to begin for a full year. While developing their plans, with famed English architect William Quayle, Louis put out his first ever advertisement.12

First advertisement for “Louis’ Bakery and Confectionery”, December 2nd, 1880.13

Just two months later Louis would sell his store along with the building, its fixtures, and the entire stock of groceries.14 He would then move into a smaller rented space inside the Old City bakery owned by D. L. Dawley.15 This all occurred a month before construction began on Louis’s proposed business block, and was likely done to finance the undertaking.

Excavations began March 1881 for a building fifty feet wide and seventy feet long.16 A stone foundation and basement were put in place.17 By June, the Golden pressed brick walls reached the second floor and a month later an elegant zinc belt course complemented the facade.1819 In September, French plate glass 64×124 inches in size was fitted to the Norman arched windows.20 As a final touch the front was ornamented with galvanized iron columns.21 By October the building was at completion and officially being called the “Reed-Dauth Block.”

Despite previously stating that he intended to use the building for own use Louis would rent out his side of the property to other tenets for a full year. During this time he moved his business back into the original building he used when he first came to Fort Collins, four years prior.22

 

 

New Store!!

Opening of Louis Dauth’s new Grocery and Bakery

I Would respectfully inform the public that I have opened my new grocery and bakery on Linden street, south of Mrs. Yount’s bank, where I keep a full stock of groceries, candies, oysters, tobacco, &c. I would be glad to receive the patronage of my old friends and the public generally. Very Respectfully, Louis Dauth23

Express – October 20, 1881

The first tenets of the Reed-Dauth block on the first floor were the E. W. Reed jewelry store and the Boston Square-Dealing clothing store. On the second floor was the Fort Collins Courier newspaper as well as two offices for surgeon-dentist Henry Cone and lawyer J. W. Norvell.

Advertisements for the first tenets of the Reed-Dauth block. On the main floor were the Reed jewelry store (top left) and the Square Dealing House clothing store (bottom left).2425 On the second floor were the Fort Collins Courier newspaper (top right), lawyer J. W. Norvel (middle right) and surgeon-dentist Henry Cone (bottom right).262728

After a little more than a year the Boston Square Dealing house ended their lease and Louis began preparing this vacant space for his new business.29 The walls and ceiling were calcimined, awnings were put in place, new counters and shelving were put in, a thirty ton ice house was built, an elevator was added to the cellar, and a gold gilded sign adorned the entrance.30313233 Louis was said to have the finest store in town.34

In the past year Louis had been experimenting with new types of merchandise, and was looking to expand his product line even further.

Louis’s first advertisement for his business in the Reed-Dauth block, May 8th 1883.35

Louis no longer referred to his business a bakery and instead called it a “Fancy Grocery.” After a few months Louis entered a new partnership with A. E. Annis who agreed to purchase half interest in the store.36

Advertisements for the Louis Dauth’s business on June 14 1883 (left), and on June 28 1883 (right) after partnering with A. E. Annis.3738

Photograph of Linden Street, taken by E. W. Reed, part owner of the Reed-Dauth block, c. 1884.39  The Reed-Dauth block is the building with an awning directly behind the large street clock in the center of the photo.

Closeup of the Reed-Dauth block, c. 1884.40 Louis Dauth owned the southern half of the building (left side) and E. W. Reed owned the northern half (right side).41 The Fort Collins Courier newspaper was located on the upper floor of the northern half while their printing press was in the basement.42 The upper floors of the southern half held offices.43 The clock tower was likely installed by Reed as an advertisement for the watches and clocks he sold.

Louis was always on the lookout for new product and the newspapers were constantly reporting on the high quality or unique goods found at his store:

Newspaper articles commenting on the quality of Dauth & Annis potatoes brought in from Wyoming and apples shipped all the way from Michigan and Missouri.4445

The partnership with A. E. Annis lasted until Annis decided to retire, on February 5th 1885.46 Louis continued to run the store with help from his youngest brother George Dauth, who had just arrived from Germany. To improve sales Louis tried to find novel ways to promote his merchandise.  

Louis Dauth the popular Linden street grocer, gave a coffee festival Monday afternoon and Tuesday at his store for the purpose of introducing Chase & Sanborn’s famous coffees. Great numbers took advantage of the opportunity offered and indulged in a cup of very fine coffee made in an elegant silver urn standing on the counter.47

Fort Collins Courier – May 5th 1887

Louis Dauth has arranged a battery of Fourth of July ordinance in one of his show windows and we shall expect to see Linden street raked fore and aft with chain shot on Independence day.48

Fort Collins Courier – June 16th 1887

Business was booming for Louis. He was constantly having to add more capacity to his store and reportedly had so many customers a cashier had to be hired that did “nothing but receive cash and make change.”4950 Louis had become a big fish in a small pond, and was looking for new waters.

In February 1888 he sold all of his property in Fort Collins and moved to Five Points, Denver.51 Here he would continue his business eventually becoming the owner of several stores, a saloon, and numerous homes. Despite never returning to Fort Collins and only being here for a decade, Louis had left a small legacy here, as an example of how hard work and ambition can pay off.

The Reed-Dauth block still exist today at 233 Linden Street, and Louis Dauth’s store is now a restaurant. Stop by have a bite and see the 140 year facade and original iron columns.

1    Dauth, Louis, Corbett, Hoye & Co.’s Annual Denver City Directory, 1874, Denver Public Library
2    Dauth, Louis, Corbett, Hoye & Co.’s Annual Denver City Directory, 1875, Denver Public Library
3    Dauth, Louis, Corbett, Hoye & Co.’s Annual Denver City Directory, 1877, Denver Public Library
4    Dauth, Louis, Corbett, Hoye & Co.’s Annual Denver City Directory, 1876, Denver Public Library

item.title: George Dauth’s Meat Market
item.dc:creator: Nathaniel Dauth
item.description: The post George Dauth’s Meat Market appeared first on Dauth Family Archive.
item.content:encoded:
George Dauth’s Meat Market

806 9th Street, Greeley

George Dauth was youngest son of Andreas Dauth, who immigrated to America in 1884 at the age of 15.1 When he arrived he began working for his eldest brother Louis, at the “Dauth & Annis” grocery in Fort Collins.2 He eventually set off on his own in 1888 and found his way to Leadville, where he apprenticed in butchery at E. J. Van Camp’s Monarch Grocery.3 During this time Louis sold his Fort Collins business and moved to Five Points in Denver to open a new grocery. George wanting to support his brother moved to Denver in 1894 to work as a butcher at Louis’s new location.4 George would practice his trade here for the next decade, transitioning from a butcher to a clerk and eventually owning his own share of Louis’s business.

In 1903 George married Florence Yeaton and together they decided to move Greeley where Florence had plans to open a Millinery Parlor.5 Within a month of moving George had announced his intentions to open his own business, and by October of 1903 had secured a lease to rent the first floor of a new building being built on the corner of Eight avenue and Ninth street.6 After several months of preparations George announced the opening of the “George Dauth & Company” meat market at 806 Ninth Street on January 7th 1904.7

Announcement advertisement for George Dauth’s meat market, “Geo. Dauth & Co.” on January 7th, 1904.

George initially advertised for the sale of fresh, salted, and smoked meats along with oysters and fish. He ordered corn fed cattle by the train load and rendered lard in-house at the grocery.8

Advertisement for George Dauth’s “Ninth Street Market”9

In 1905, George hung a giant five by seven foot painting inside his store to attract customers. It was painted by his mother-in-law Mrs. Zelotes Yeaton a professional artist based in Oklahoma. Local newspapers claimed it was the largest painting in Greeley at the time.

Article describing George’s giant cattle painting by the Greeley Tribune, November 23rd 1905.10

Photograph of George Dauth’s store painting, gifted to him by his mother in law Mrs. Zelotes Yeaton. It’s last known location was in a storage facility owned by the University of Northern Colorado.11

In 1908 George ran a five month long advertisement campaign, publishing simple meat based recipes weekly. He hoped that the new “unusual” recipes would catch the readers’ eyes and attract new customers.

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

Recipe Page

By 1909 George had acquired ranch land, located on Fifth Street next to property owned by W. H. Senier, which he would use to raise livestock and run a slaughter house.1213 He further expanded his business in 1911 with a new barn located just behind the store.14 George became Increasingly interested in the livestock side of the butchery business and in 1913 sold the Ninth Street Market to completely shift his focus towards ranching.15

A view of Ninth Street in Greeley in 1914. George Dauth’s Meat Market is the second shortest building located between the two taller buildings at the end of the block.16

A closeup of the above photo shows the “M” and “E” of the “Meat Market” painted sign on the buildings western wall.

For five years George raised and sold livestock while increasingly becoming more involved with Greeley’s social organizations, such as the Elks and Commercial Clubs.17 However George realized that he was missing the day to day routine of running a store and so in April 1918 he bought back the old Ninth Street Market from S. Reed.18

George Dauth driving his car behind the Ahlstrand Real Estate Building c. 1920. George Dauth’s store is just out of frame to the right of the C. T. Ahlstrand sign.

In 1920 George purchased the Idlewild Lodge in the Big Thompson Canyon.19 His focus shifted completely towards developing the property and making it into a summertime retreat. To give him more time in the mountains he sought out help at the store, which is when met August and Henry Baab. The Baab Brother’s agreed to go into business with George in 1921.20

The Baab Bros & Dauth Market & Grocery at 806 9th Street, Greeley. George is standing at left, his son George “June” second from left, and one of the Baab’s brothers August Baab is third from left.

The new partnership brought about a new name for the grocery and it was henceforth known as Baab Brothers & Dauth. During the 1920s George was spending most of his time and energy developing Idlewild Lodge, with a smaller role at the store.

A view of Ninth Street in Greeley in 1929.21 The Baabs Brothers and Dauth Market is the third building from the left corner.

Closeup of the the Baab Bros & Dauth building. The painted logo of C. T. Ahlstrand Real Estate is visible two buildings over.

In 1930 George decided to finally purchase the building that the grocery had been occupying since 1903.22 Up to this point he had been leasing the space from Charles Lofgren, who originally built the building. This acquisition signaled a return of George to the day to day activities at the store.  New windows and shelves were installed, decorations were put up, and new items were brought in for sale.2324 The Greeley Tribune commented on the novel and modern “Self Serve” experience now offered at Baabs Brothers & Dauth.25 George had also acquired a fish hatchery and began advertising for “Fresh Caught Rainbow Trout.” In an effort to get set the store name mentioned in the papers the son of George Dauth, June, started a softball team named “Baab Bros & Dauth” that competed in the City Softball League.26 Also beginning in 1931 was an ambitious weekly advertisement campaign, where ads were were placed in the Greeley Tribune showcasing the weeks door busters.

1933-06-23 – Greeley Daily Tribune – Baab Bros and Dauth Advertisement

Details

1931-02-13 – Greeley Daily Tribune – Baab Bros and Dauth Advertisement

Details

In 1932 a delivery service was started where anyone could call in and have groceries delivered to their home. George Dauth’s son, June, was the primary driver.27 In May of 1932 June fell ill, and unexpectedly passed away. The following year, in 1933, George would sell his share of the company to the Baab Brothers and retire. He had been in the business for twenty three years.28

The Baab Brothers would continue to run the grocery under the same name until 1935.29 The building was then leased to George Ernst who started his own grocery and market there.30

806 Ninth Street still exist today and George Dauth’s old store is now a restaurant. Stop by have a bite and see the original “MEAT MARKET” painted brick wall that is still visible on west side of the building.

1    “George Dauth Killed by Fast Train at Evans.” Weekly Tribune [Greeley, CO], 29 Apr. 1946
2    “George Dauth Killed by Fast Train at Evans.” Weekly Tribune [Greeley, CO], 29 Apr. 1946
